title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates why foundation models like CLIP exhibit strong out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. The authors highlight that CLIP achieves notably higher zero-shot accuracy on OOD benchmarks than models trained purely on labeled in-distribution (ID) data (e.g., ImageNet) and then evaluated OOD (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch). Given the sheer scale of CLIP’s pretraining corpus, it is plausible that it contains many images similar to those in OOD test sets, raising the question of whether these benchmarks are truly OOD for CLIP and whether such overlap explains its performance.

To probe this, the authors operationalize perceptual similarity using embeddings from a pretrained CLIP ViT-B/16. They show that common OOD datasets (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) share more close neighbors with LAION than with ImageNet.

Their first experiment iteratively removes LAION samples that are most similar to the OOD test images and retrains models on the pruned data. As anticipated, performance declines as similar images are removed, indicating that training examples close to the test distribution contribute meaningfully to OOD accuracy.

The second experiment contrasts models trained on a small labeled dataset (ImageNet) with those trained on LAION. The authors define a “generalization gap” metric based on, for each test image, the minimum distance to any training image. They prune LAION so that its generalization gap relative to the OOD test set matches that of ImageNet, then train CLIP on this pruned subset. Although this induces some performance drop, the resulting model still substantially outperforms one trained solely on ImageNet. From this, they infer that CLIP’s robustness is driven primarily by data scale rather than merely by train–test similarity.

Strengths:
- Clear writing and presentation.
- Experiments are well described with explicit motivations.

Weaknesses:
- All analyses center on ImageNet and its variants. The absence of results on other OOD settings (e.g., iWildCam, FMoW) leaves open whether the conclusions generalize beyond this family of datasets.

Questions:
1. Do you have results on datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Including such experiments would considerably strengthen the argument.
2. Why restrict filtering to CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings? Would larger CLIP models yield better similarity estimates for pruning?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper examines CLIP’s generalization through the lens of the training data. While CLIP is renowned for strong generalization, it is unclear whether this is simply due to web-scale datasets containing many samples similar to evaluation sets. The authors address this by removing training examples that are nearest neighbors to test data in CLIP embedding space. By constructing a LAION subset that exhibits a train–test generalization gap comparable to that of the ImageNet-1K training set, they argue that CLIP’s excellent out-of-distribution (OOD) performance is not primarily driven by having already seen near-duplicate or closely distributed samples, but by other factors.

Strengths:
1. The paper offers a novel angle on CLIP’s OOD generalization by focusing on train–test distribution similarity, yielding interesting insights—for instance, that a 100M subset of LAION-400M can train a model that performs well on OOD benchmarks.
2. The experimental setup is thoughtfully motivated and executed, producing persuasive evidence that CLIP maintains strong OOD performance even after removing nearest-neighbor training samples.

Weaknesses:
1. The study is limited to standard classification-oriented OOD benchmarks and overlooks other key CLIP use cases. Retrieval, in particular, is a core evaluation scenario. The authors should consider using MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for filtering and Flickr30k as the OOD dataset. Broader evaluation on diverse suites such as VTAB (see the DataComp paper for current best practices) is also encouraged.
2. Several important experimental details are missing. For example, Section 4.1 does not clearly describe how the nearest-neighbor sets are constructed (if this is only in the appendix, it should be surfaced). Please specify how many samples are selected and what CLIP similarity thresholds are used.
3. The paper should compare the distribution of neighbor similarities to the test sets between the full LAION dataset (after the filtering in Sections 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could contend that even if the single closest sample in LAION and ImageNet-Train are at similar distances to the test set, LAION might still have far more samples clustered near that closest point (though not as close to the test set as the nearest one), providing an inherent advantage on OOD benchmarks and weakening the strength of the conclusions.

Questions:
What are the practical implications of these findings? I recognize this is primarily an analytical study and believe it clears the ICLR acceptance bar. Still, I would like to hear more about the broader impact—how these results could guide future research directions or inform engineering practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates whether the strong OOD performance of foundation models such as CLIP stems from the training set containing images that closely resemble the OOD test data. To probe this, the authors construct multiple filtered splits of LAION (the dataset used to train OpenCLIP). They show that removing training samples highly similar to the OOD test sets leads to a notable performance drop on those OOD benchmarks. However, when the train–test similarity is calibrated to match that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, CLIP still substantially outperforms a model pretrained on ImageNet. Hence, while LAION does include images resembling ImageNet-based OOD test sets, this does not fully explain CLIP’s superior OOD generalization. The underlying cause of CLIP’s better generalization remains unresolved.

Strengths:
- The core result—that reducing train–test similarity does not eliminate CLIP’s advantage and even yields improvements relative to an ImageNet baseline under matched similarity—is valuable.
- The paper presents a suite of thoughtful, computationally demanding experiments that will be useful to the community.
- The released code and pruned datasets offer a practical resource for continued study of CLIP’s OOD behavior.

Weaknesses:
- The main limitation is that the current analysis does not establish a truly fair OOD evaluation. For in-distribution (ID) settings, removing training samples near each test example could suffice. But in an OOD context, the test distribution is unknown; thus, to ensure fairness, all images from the test domain should be excluded, not just those similar to specific test instances. As a result, even with the proposed pruning, the claim of “out-of-distribution generalization” is not warranted.
- Concretely, if there are only a handful of “airplane” sketches in the test set, the method removes training images close to those particular sketches. Yet other airplane sketches—potentially farther than the closest ImageNet test image—may remain in training. Therefore, it is not ensured that all instances from the relevant classes and the test domain are fully removed.
- Additionally, objects absent from ImageNet, such as “space shuttle,” could appear in the training data as sketches (and natural images). This means the “sketch” domain is not truly unseen to CLIP, undermining the validity of the OOD evaluation.

Minor feedback:
- The abstract and contributions could be made crisper; a more concise summary of the main contributions would improve clarity.
- Consider renaming “generalization gap,” as this term commonly refers to a different concept.

Typo:
- “We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and ImageNet”

Questions/requests for additional experiments:
- To approximate a genuine OOD setup, further experiments are needed as outlined above. For instance, when evaluating on ImageNet-Sketch, one should also remove all sketch-style images from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned). Otherwise, remaining sketch images in training can reduce the domain discrepancy between sketches and natural images, compromising the fairness of the OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper investigates whether CLIP’s test-time accuracy largely arises from the presence of highly similar images in its training data. The authors use LAION as the training source, identify and remove samples that are close to various OOD benchmarks, retrain CLIP, and then measure the resulting accuracy drop. In Section 4.1, they report that for certain OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R), LAION contains more—and more semantically and stylistically aligned—neighbors to the OOD images than the ImageNet training set does. They also find a positive relationship between accuracy and the presence of similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. The remainder of the work focuses on pruning LAION of samples near specific OOD datasets and evaluating models trained on these pruned versions.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 compellingly illustrates that LAION-400M includes images that are both semantically and stylistically close to OOD benchmarks, unlike the ImageNet training set.
- Figure 3 quantitatively demonstrates that nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are, on average, more similar than nearest neighbors drawn from ImageNet-train, with the exception of the ImageNet-Val set itself. The right panel of Fig. 3 also clearly indicates that CLIP achieves higher accuracy on test samples for which similar training images existed.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val yields a substantial accuracy decline relative to pruning random samples, underscoring that these near samples are key contributors to CLIP’s effective robustness.

Weaknesses:
- While Figure 4 suggests that near-pruned samples are critical for CLIP’s OOD performance, the evidence is not definitive. Two additional experiments would strengthen the claims:
i) Determine whether the performance degradation is specific to the pruned benchmark or reflects a broader loss in classification capability. For instance, plot accuracies on ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and after removing samples close to ImageNet-Sketch, demonstrate that only ImageNet-Sketch performance declines.
ii) Assess whether CLIP’s OOD generalization deteriorates faster under pruning than a standard ImageNet-trained classifier. This requires pruning ImageNet with an analogous procedure by X% and comparing the accuracy decay; if degradation rates are comparable, it would imply that observed OOD generalization may simply track the presence of similar training instances.

- The paper is well-positioned to engage more deeply with definitions of “zero-shot” and “OOD robustness,” and this discussion currently feels underdeveloped. After showing that pruning harms OOD performance, the implications for evaluation practice remain unclear. Should we conclude that CLIP’s zero-shot performance is compromised by training-set leakage of similar images? Similarly, the motivation behind Sections 5 and 6—“Correcting for highly similar images”—needs clarification. Why should we deliberately prune training data to reduce performance on benchmarks when exact test samples are not duplicated in the training set? What is the intended value of the pruned models in Table 1 that underperform?

Questions:
- Regarding Figure 4: What happens if the same pruning approach is applied to models trained on ImageNet? Do we observe analogous accuracy drops?
- Figure 7 indicates that removing samples close to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val degrades accuracy across other ImageNet OOD benchmarks as well. Does this imply these samples are broadly important for capturing the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this approach, and any model trained on such corrected datasets, be considered transductive learning since the test sets are directly or indirectly used? Would this contravene licenses like ObjectNet’s stipulation that “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: “Let us for consider” → “Let us consider”

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

See weakness above.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a subgoal-based planning approach built on diffusion models. Building on Diffuser [1], which generates a full trajectory of states and actions from start to goal, the authors propose a two-level scheme: a high-level diffuser that proposes a sequence of subgoals, and a low-level diffuser that plans the state-action trajectory for each subgoal segment. Experiments span Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym, and FrankKitchen, with comparisons against relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL baselines. The results indicate better “receptibility,” stronger out-of-distribution generalization, and reduced computational cost relative to Diffuser, supported by ablations.

Strengths:
- Decomposing planning into subgoals within a diffusion framework appears beneficial, enabling coverage of a broader range of scenarios, and making the low-level diffusion planner largely task-agnostic.
- The sparse diffuser (SD) variant outperforms the original Diffuser thanks to an enlarged receptive field. The paper corroborates this by showing that increasing Diffuser’s kernel size improves performance but harms generalization.
- Combining SD with dense actions improves optimization of the sparse objective, addressing a common pain point in hierarchical RL.
- HD is faster than Diffuser because the high-level sequences are shorter and the low-level segment plans can be solved in parallel.

Weaknesses:
- The conceptual novelty is limited. Recognizing that a single diffuser struggles with long horizons, the approach introduces two diffusers: a sparse, high-level subgoal planner from start to goal, and a standard diffuser for each subgoal segment.
- Since there exist state-only diffusion methods such as Decision-Diffuser [2], the paper should include ablations clarifying why state-only modeling is insufficient for accurately estimating J. Also, if the implementation follows Diffuser’s code, are actions in Maze2D sampled from the diffusion model or produced by a separate controller as in Diffuser?
- Planning each subgoal segment independently, without explicit conditioning on the ultimate goal, risks suboptimality. How is overlap between segment paths avoided? How many trajectory samples are drawn per segment and for the full subgoal sequence?
- In maze settings, what occurs if the executed actions fail to hit the exact subgoal (unless inverse-dynamics actions are used)? Without feedback from the low-level diffuser to the high-level diffuser, this can be problematic.

Overall, this is a strong refinement of Diffuser with improvements across several dimensions. Nonetheless, the incremental nature of the contributions tempers the significance.

Questions:
See weaknesses above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.

Strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.

Weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a hierarchical extension of Diffuser. A coarse high-level planner proposes sparse trajectories, and a fine-grained low-level planner fills in detailed states along those trajectories. Empirically, the hierarchical approach improves performance on long-horizon planning and offline RL benchmarks. The authors also analyze how kernel size in Diffuser and the use of jumpy steps affect outcomes. Notably, they find that a small receptive field leads to poor coverage of the underlying data distribution.

Strengths:
1. The manuscript is clearly written and easy to follow.
2. The approach is simple yet effective, yielding strong gains on long-horizon tasks, with especially strong results on AntMaze.
3. The investigation into how the diffusion model’s receptive field relates to data coverage is insightful and can inform future work on diffusion-based planning in more complex settings.

Weaknesses:
- A minor shortcoming is the absence of the Decision Diffuser [1] baseline. Moreover, the analysis centers on the CNN-based Diffuser architecture; a comparison against a transformer-based alternative, such as Decision Diffuser, would be valuable.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. Referring to Fig. 3, when using a small kernel, Diffuser covers only a single mode during actual execution. Does the standard Diffuser manage to cover the full data distribution in principle but fail to realize full coverage at execution time? If yes, what explains this discrepancy?
2. Also in Fig. 3, while HD appears to capture most of the distribution, it misses two short segments/branches. What drives this behavior?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.

Strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.

Weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.

Questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.
reviews.questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.

Strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.

Weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.

Questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffuser, a straightforward yet effective hierarchical planning method built on diffusion models to tackle long-horizon tasks more efficiently and accurately. The approach uses a two-level architecture: a high-level component, the Sparse Diffuser, to propose sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine and execute the plan. Empirically, it clearly outperforms non-hierarchical diffusion-based planners and reduces computational cost for both training and inference. It also shows stronger generalization on out-of-distribution tasks than non-hierarchical Diffusers.

Strengths:
- Originality: The work thoughtfully combines hierarchical planning with diffusion models, yielding notable gains in both performance and computational efficiency relative to prior methods.
- Quality: The paper provides solid theoretical grounding alongside carefully designed experiments and thorough analysis. The design choices are well motivated, and their benefits are convincingly discussed.
- Clarity: The manuscript is well structured and clearly written, with an accessible explanation of the method, its strengths, and its limitations.

Weaknesses:
- Given the prior HDMI algorithm, the incremental contribution here is somewhat constrained. That said, this paper goes further than HDMI in its breadth of generalization experiments and offers theoretical backing. I recommend the authors foreground the generalization advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser and explicitly emphasize generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In hierarchical setups, the quality of high-level goal proposals is pivotal. While the authors argue their method is simpler than other hierarchical approaches, they commit to only one goal selection strategy. Beyond the fixed time-interval heuristic used here, HDMI discusses two additional simple and low-cost heuristics based on spatial intervals and reward scales. Incorporating these alternatives would strengthen the paper’s completeness. Moreover, the paper lacks a clear demonstration of the quality of the generated goals, which is important and currently missing.

Questions:
- Diffuser and Decision Diffuser use “inpainting” for generating trajectories in goal-conditioned tasks (as does the lower-level planner in HDMI), whereas this paper adopts return-guided sampling. What motivated this choice between inpainting and return-guided sampling?
- The paper first extracts sub-goals via a planning-based subgoal extractor and then trains HDMI with these sub-goals as supervision. While this two-stage approach is reasonable, it makes the overall training pipeline feel ad hoc and less general. Is there any coupling between the high- and low-level planners in Hierarchical Diffuser? Can the system be trained end-to-end?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The Hierarchical Diffuser incorporates a two-tiered approach: a high-level "jumpy" planning strategy that can quickly survey a broader scope of possibilities with reduced computational demands, and a low-level planner that refines these broader plans into specific, actionable steps. This hierarchy allows the method to operate more efficiently and effectively, with the higher level providing guidance that simplifies the task for the lower level. Empirical evaluations show that this method outperforms both traditional diffusion-based planners and other hierarchical approaches in speed and performance on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks.

This paper proposed a modified version of the diffuser called Hierarchical Diffuser.  The Hierarchical Diffuser incorporates a two-tiered approach: a high-level "jumpy" planning strategy that can quickly survey a broader scope of possibilities with reduced computational demands, and a low-level planner that refines these broader plans into specific, actionable steps. This hierarchy allows the method to operate more efficiently and effectively, with the higher level providing guidance that simplifies the task for the lower level. Empirical evaluations show that this method outperforms both traditional diffusion-based planners and other hierarchical approaches in speed and performance on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks.

Strengths:
- Using a hierarchical structure makes sense in long-horizon planning. 
- In replanning, a hierarchical structure is more efficient since it only needs to use low-level to 
- Show the relationship between kernel size and generalization.

Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a variant of diffusion-based planning dubbed Hierarchical Diffuser. The method employs a two-level scheme: a high-level, jumpy planner that rapidly explores coarse trajectories with lower computational cost, and a low-level controller that translates these coarse plans into concrete actions. This design leverages top-down guidance to simplify the low-level task, aiming for both greater efficiency and effectiveness. Experiments indicate improved speed and performance relative to standard diffusion planners and prior hierarchical baselines on common offline RL benchmarks.

Strengths:
- A hierarchical formulation is a natural fit for long-horizon planning.
- For replanning, a hierarchical setup can be more efficient because it only needs to use the low-level component.
- The paper examines how kernel size relates to generalization.

Weaknesses:
- Recent SOTA diffusers still appear stronger; for instance, [1] reports a score of 167 on large maze2d.
- U-Net architectures are already hierarchical; if an environment benefits from hierarchy, simply deepening the U-Net is a straightforward alternative. More results are needed to convincingly demonstrate superiority over such baselines.
- The gains on Mujoco are not compelling enough for me.
- Although the paper claims to test generalization, the OOD scenarios are too simple. Other works use more challenging OOD settings (e.g., [1] adding coins; [2] adding obstacles).
- For hierarchical methods, smoothness at the interfaces between levels is crucial, but the paper does not address this issue.

Questions:
- The role of SD-DA is unclear. It seems to help only on walker2d. Predicting very fine-grained actions appears to undercut the benefits of high-level planning.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Decision:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: NECO: NEural Collapse Based Out-of-distribution detection

reviews.summary: In this paper, a novel post-hoc OOD detection method, NECO, is presented. NECO utilizes the geometric properties of neural collapse (NC) and principal component space to identify OOD data. This paper hypothesizes that NC not only affects the convergence of the loss during training but also impacts OOD detection.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper is clearly motivated and well-written.
2. This paper is innovative in the novel observation of ID/OOD orthogonality(NC5).
3. This paper provides thorough theoretical analysis.
4. Experimental results on the ImageNet and CIFAR benchmarks demonstrate that NECO is state-of-the-art. And NECO can be generalized to various backbones.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The NECO score is similar to the ViM score. The main difference is that the NECO score utilizes principal space features, while the ViM score utilizes null space features for OOD detection.
2. The manuscript lacks critical baseline comparisons, such as [1]. It is recommended that the authors conduct a comparison with it to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.

[1] Song, Yue, Nicu Sebe, and Wei Wang. "Rankfeat: Rank-1 feature removal for out-of-distribution detection." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 17885-17898.
reviews.questions: Why not put NuSA in the baseline? Can NECO outperform NuSA?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, a novel post-hoc OOD detection method, NECO, is presented. NECO utilizes the geometric properties of neural collapse (NC) and principal component space to identify OOD data. This paper hypothesizes that NC not only affects the convergence of the loss during training but also impacts OOD detection.

Strengths:
1. This paper is clearly motivated and well-written.
2. This paper is innovative in the novel observation of ID/OOD orthogonality(NC5).
3. This paper provides thorough theoretical analysis.
4. Experimental results on the ImageNet and CIFAR benchmarks demonstrate that NECO is state-of-the-art. And NECO can be generalized to various backbones.

Weaknesses:
1. The NECO score is similar to the ViM score. The main difference is that the NECO score utilizes principal space features, while the ViM score utilizes null space features for OOD detection.
2. The manuscript lacks critical baseline comparisons, such as [1]. It is recommended that the authors conduct a comparison with it to provide a more comprehensive evaluation.

[1] Song, Yue, Nicu Sebe, and Wei Wang. "Rankfeat: Rank-1 feature removal for out-of-distribution detection." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 17885-17898.

Questions:
Why not put NuSA in the baseline? Can NECO outperform NuSA?

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work introduces NECO, a post-hoc OOD detection approach that leverages the geometric structure associated with neural collapse (NC) and the principal component subspace of features. The central claim is that NC influences not just training loss convergence but also provides a useful signal for distinguishing OOD samples.

Strengths:
- The paper is clearly motivated and well-presented.
- It offers an original insight into ID/OOD orthogonality (NC5).
- Theoretical analysis is comprehensive.
- Experiments on ImageNet and CIFAR show state-of-the-art performance, and the method transfers well across different backbone architectures.

Weaknesses:
- The NECO scoring mechanism bears strong resemblance to ViM; the key distinction is that NECO relies on features in the principal subspace, whereas ViM uses the null space for OOD detection.
- Important baselines are missing, notably RankFeat [1]. Including such comparisons would strengthen the empirical evaluation and provide a fairer benchmark.

[1] Song, Yue, Nicu Sebe, and Wei Wang. "Rankfeat: Rank-1 feature removal for out-of-distribution detection." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 17885-17898.

Questions:
- Why is NuSA not included among the baselines?
- Does NECO outperform NuSA?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Decision:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to study OOD detection from the perspective of Neural Collapse. In that regard, they proposed NECO, Neural Collapse-based OOD Detection. NECO involves calculating the relative norm of a sample within the subspace configuration adopted by the ID data (i.e., Simplex ETF). The authors show that NECO exhibits strong generalization capabilities across various network architectures.
reviews.strengths: This paper is overall well-written although the wording can somewhat be simplified (see weaknesses). The idea is quite simple and intuitive. The method is thoroughly evaluated and shows great performance against many benchmark datasets and OOD detection methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. At times, the manuscript is a bit misleading. For instance, the sentence "Intuitively, these properties depicts the tendency of the network to maximally separate class features while minimizing the separation within them" is quite counter-intuitive. I'd suggest the authors to proofread the manuscript again to make it easier to digest.

2. Although NECO shows strong ID classification and OOD detection capabilities across aggregated benchmarks, it undeperformson standalone datasets such as ImageNet-O, Textures, and iNaturalist depending on the encoding architecture that is used. An explanation for this behavior could make a stronger case for the paper.

3. Since NECO is a post-hoc method, it is not obvious from the manuscript at what point in the training process does the neural collapse occurs for NECO to kick in. In other words, a model could still be well-trained without necessarily reaching the neural collapse phase. In that specific case, would NECO require training the model a bit more, or how would NECO's performance compare with say NuSA or ViM?

4. NECO makes a somewhat stringent assumption that the subspace of the ID (and OOD?) is linear by making use of PCA to learn the matrix $P$ spanned by the d eigenvectors corresponding to the largest d eigenvalues of the covariance matrix $H$, where $H$ represents the feature space of the ID data. I think a justification as to why this feature space is linear is important. A non-linear dimensionality reduction, or a manifold-based learning method, would be more appropriate given the fact that neural nets are non-linear in nature.
reviews.questions: Please refer to the weaknesses I highlighted in the above section.
